input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin (PT) is an incubator used to heat a styrofoam‑enclosed box to a target temperature and keep it stable independent of the box contents. The plant consists of a heat‑bed as the heating element, a fan for air circulation, two internal temperature sensors and one external temperature sensor. The heating plant is driven by a Raspberry Pi acting as the controller. The system operates in the temperature‑regulation domain of a cyber‑physical system, where the goal is to achieve and maintain a setpoint temperature inside the insulated container.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin supplies (i) real‑time monitoring of the temperature sensor streams, (ii) safety and optimality checks through monitors that raise violations, (iii) simulation‑based prediction of the incubator’s thermal behaviour under current operating conditions, (iv) optimisation of system parameters via calibration algorithms that update the physical and cyber models, (v) advanced visualisation dashboards for engineers/operators to inspect sensor data, model outputs and prediction results, and (vi) reconfigurability support that allows the twin to adapt its models when the environment or operating conditions change, thereby improving robustness and reducing maintenance actions.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation of the DT relies on the Raspberry Pi as the embedded controller for the PT, the temperature sensors for data acquisition, and a publicly available GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) that hosts the hardware and software specifications, model code and calibration routines. The DT uses calibration algorithms to update the physical and environmental models with parameter estimates derived from the sensor data; these algorithms, together with monitoring and optimisation modules, constitute the software enablers for the DT.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin follows a CPS‑centric orchestration pattern where sensor data from the Physical Twin are continuously streamed into the cyber side. Calibration algorithms consume this data to update the parameters of physics‑based models of the system and its environment. Updated model outputs are fed to monitoring components that assess safety and performance criteria. If a violation is detected, a decision layer—potentially involving human operators or automated supervisory controllers—triggers corrective actions that may be sent back to the Physical Twin. Thus the architecture integrates data acquisition, model calibration, monitoring, and decision/actuation loops within a unified framework.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin incorporates two analytical thermal simulation models of the incubator: a two‑parameter model and a more detailed four‑parameter model that captures the heatbed’s thermal inertia. Both models are behavioral/analytical representations that predict the internal temperature of the insulated box. Data streams consist of temperature measurements collected over time from sensors embedded in the physical incubator. These sensor data are fed to calibration algorithms that estimate the free parameters of each model, thereby keeping the models synchronised with the Physical Twin. The models and data are linked in the DT framework so that updated model predictions can drive monitors and optimisation routines.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical twin’s actuation is provided by an electrically powered heat‑bed that generates heat inside the insulated incubator. Actuation is commanded by the system controller (the Raspberry Pi) through a simple on/off (bang‑bang) control strategy described in the case study. Commands are transmitted over the same communication channel used for sensor data. The controller implements a “safe protocol” that ensures the heat‑bed is not driven beyond its designed operating limits, thereby providing a safety constraint for the actuation commands.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Temperature sensing is performed by three Maxim Integrated DS18S20 digital temperature sensors placed at different vertical positions inside the incubator. Each sensor measures –55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of ±0.5 °C and a resolution of 0.5 °C. The sensors are read by the microcontroller at the controller’s sampling interval and the readings are transmitted over the same communication channel used for actuation (e.g., MQTT messages). The data are timestamped and stored for model calibration and monitoring. No additional sensing modalities are described.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity was assessed experimentally by comparing predicted temperature trajectories against measured values. The four‑parameter model demonstrated superior accuracy in reproducing the heatbed’s thermal inertia and was therefore selected for further DT development. Calibration algorithms are used to update model parameters based on incoming sensor data, providing a basic validation loop. Explicit uncertainty quantification, error detection mechanisms, or broader quality‑assurance processes are not described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data are collected from the physical twin through sensors over time; the sensor measurements constitute the raw input that the Digital Twin ingests for monitoring, model calibration, and optimisation.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Algorithms within the Digital Twin generate control actions when monitors detect violations; these actions may be executed by humans or automated components to modify the physical twin’s operation.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin continuously runs calibrated simulations of the Physical Twin using the integrated models and sensor data. Simulation outcomes are processed by monitoring algorithms that evaluate safety and optimality constraints. When a constraint violation is detected, the DT raises an alert and suggests a corrective action. Thus, decision support is provided through predictive insights (e.g., anticipated temperature evolution), safety insights (fault or out‑of‑range detection), and performance insights (deviation from optimal operating points). Human operators—or higher‑level control software—receive these insights through the monitoring interface and decide whether to modify actuator commands such as heater set‑points or fan speed. The documents do not specify quantitative confidence measures for the recommendations.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.


3 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


3


Insulated

Container







Temperature







Controller


Figure 2: Schematic overview of the Incubator.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction provides a highly accurate and detailed description of the incubator system, including its components, goals, and domain. It correctly identifies the styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, sensors (two internal, one external), and the Raspberry Pi controller, as well as the goal to regulate temperature regardless of content. This is strongly supported by the source text. Quotes: 'main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and regulate it regardless of the content inside', 'plant of the Incubator consists of... A styrofoam box... A heatbed... A fan... Two temperature sensors... One temperature sensor', 'controlled by a Raspberry Pi'.

The extraction effectively synthesizes the Digital Twin services described in the Introduction, including monitoring, safety checks, simulation, optimization, visualization, and reconfigurability. It correctly attributes these services to the DT framework applied to the incubator, noting capabilities like prediction and calibration. Quotes: 'DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies', 'The resulting data is then used to inform monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally.', 'DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability... safety, predictability'.

The extraction correctly identifies the Raspberry Pi as the controller, the GitHub repository for specifications, and calibration algorithms as key enablers. It accurately reflects the hardware and software setup described. However, it could be expanded to include other specific tools mentioned in the text, such as RabbitMQ for communication and the Python Scipy package for optimization. Quotes: 'Raspberry Pi 4 Model B... implement the controller', 'publicly available repository... contains the up-to-date hardware and software specifications', 'DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions that the driver 'periodically reads the temperature data' and that RabbitMQ is used for 'synchronisation', but it does not provide specific values for synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs. Therefore, the specific technical details required for this characteristic are missing, making 'Not in Document' an accurate assessment.

The document describes a single incubator physical twin and compares two simulation models (Model A and Model B) for that single system. It does not mention multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination between instances. Thus, the claim 'Not in Document' is correct.

The extraction accurately reflects the architecture described in Section 1 and Figure 1. It correctly paraphrases the text stating that 'DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models', data is used to 'inform monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally', and that 'When violations are detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical twin'. The description is highly specific and grounded in the provided text.

The document mentions hardware capabilities like Wi-Fi and Bluetooth that 'simplifies deployment' and references an external repository, but it does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards, or data exchange protocols with other systems. The focus is strictly on the internal components. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is correct.

not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).

Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that

The following important parts are highlighted [6]:

An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with

that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

block_3_judge.reasoning: The description accurately reflects the document's content regarding the two simulation models (Model A and Model B), their parameter counts, and their purpose in predicting internal temperature. The mention of 'behavioral/analytical representations' aligns with the text's derivation of differential equations. The description of data streams as temperature measurements and the use of calibration algorithms ('non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate the parameters') is well-supported. The mention of 'optimisation routines' is supported by the general definition of DT in the text which mentions 'optimisation technologies', though specific optimisation logic for the incubator is not detailed beyond calibration.

The description accurately identifies the heatbed as the actuator and the Raspberry Pi as the controller utilizing a bang-bang strategy. It correctly references the 'safe protocol' mentioned in the text for maintaining safe operating states. The details align well with the hardware and software sections. Relevant quotes: 'heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.', 'controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller', 'safe protocol is incorporated into the low-level driver'.

The extraction provides a detailed and accurate description of the DS18S20 sensors, including their count (three), range (-55 to +125°C), and accuracy (0.5°C), which are all supported by Section 2.1.5. It correctly describes the data transmission via the communication channel. However, it loses points for specifying a 'resolution of 0.5°C' (the text only specifies accuracy) and for referring to the Raspberry Pi as a 'microcontroller' (the text calls it a controller or Raspberry Pi).

The extraction correctly captures the experimental comparison between models ('experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the thermal inertia') and the use of calibration algorithms ('non-linear least squares solver'). It also accurately notes that explicit uncertainty quantification is not described. The extraction is highly specific to the text's findings.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the high-level flow of data collection for monitoring and calibration, which aligns with the introduction: 'Data – collected from the PT through sensors over time' and 'DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models'. However, it lacks key technical details present in the Source Documents, such as the specific sensor protocols ('1-Wire bus'), the communication server ('RabbitMQ'), and the periodic reading mechanism ('The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data').

The extraction correctly summarizes the control logic where 'When violations are detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical twin'. However, it is missing specific technical details from Section 2.2, such as the specific actuators ('heatbed', 'fan'), the use of RabbitMQ for message passing, and the fact that the controller is 'listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands'.

States 'Not in Document', but the Source Documents explicitly mention the technical infrastructure. This includes the 'RabbitMQ server' used for communication, support for 'Wi-Fi and Bluetooth' on the Raspberry Pi, and the '1-Wire bus' used for sensor communication.

States 'Not in Document', but the Source Documents describe the deployment setup. It specifies that the 'RabbitMQ server' is 'running on the raspberry pi itself' (on-premise/edge), the controller is on a 'Raspberry Pi 4 Model B', and mentions that RabbitMQ is 'easy to deploy on premises and in the cloud'.

We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

The document does not mention industry standards, specifications, certification requirements, or interoperability standards specific to Digital Twins. The 'Not in Document' assessment is correct.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.
